pages.text: Experimental characterization of binary and ternary blended-cement concretes containing ultraﬁne residual rice husk and sugar cane bagasse ashes G.C. Cordeiro a,⇑, R.D. Toledo Filho b,1, L.M. Tavares c,2, E.M.R. Fairbairn b,1 a Laboratory of Civil Engineering, Universidade Estadual do Norte Fluminense Darcy Ribeiro, Av. Alberto Lamego, 2000 Parque Califórnia, CEP 28013-6 02, Campos dos Goytacazes, RJ, Brazil b Program of Civil Engineering, COPPE/Universidade Federal do Rio Janeiro, Cidade Universitária UFRJ – Centro de Tecnologia Bloco I-2000 Sala 216, CE P 21941-970, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil c Program of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, COPPE/Universidade Federal do Rio Janeiro, Cidade Universitária UFRJ – Centro de Tecnologia B loco F Sala F-210, CEP 21941-972, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil article info Article history: Received 14 March 2011 Received in revised form 10 August 2011 Accepted 16 August 2011 Available online 22 December 2011 Keywords: Rice husk ash Sugar cane bagasse ash Pozzolan High-performance concrete Rheology Compressive strength abstract In the present work an experimental program was carried out to investigate the inﬂuence of residual sugar cane bagasse and rice husk ashes (SCBA and RHA, respectively) in the properties of concretes. SCBA and RHA were produced using vibratory grinding in dry open circuit and conventional and high-perfor- mance concretes were produced with 0% and 20% of both ashes as cement replacement (by mass). Ter- nary mixtures containing cement, SCBA and RHA (0.6–0.2–0.2) were also produced in order to study the beneﬁts of the simultaneous use of both ultraﬁne ashes on concrete properties. Experiments were performed to investigate the rheology (yield stress and plastic viscosity), compressive strength (at 7, 28, 90, and 180 days), Young’s modulus (at 28 days) and rapid chloride-ion penetrability. The pore size distribution and adiabatic temperature rise of the reference and ternary mixtures were also evaluated. The joint inﬂuence of both ashes allowed reaching positive effects in rheology and kept constant or increased the compressive strength when compared to the reference mixtures. Moreover, the ternary concretes presented lower electric charges and the replacement of cement by 40% SCBA and RHA decreased signiﬁcantly the maximum adiabatic temperature rise of conventional concrete. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction It has been established that mineral admixtures can be success- fully used as complementary materials in Portland cement based concretes, mortars and pastes. Several positive effects are reached by this procedure, such as improvement in mechanical properties and durability, besides reduction of heat generated during the cementitious hydration. In addition to these advantages, Portland cement replacement with by-products can cause considerable environmental beneﬁts through: (i) adequate destination of the by-products; (ii) reduction of the natural raw materials consump- tion (i.e., limestone and clay); (iii) reduction in energy consump- tion; (iv) reduction in greenhouse gas emissions. Nowadays, the last point is particularly important, considering that about 5% of anthropogenic global carbon dioxide (CO 2) emissions are gener- ated during Portland cement production [1]. Different by-products have been used as mineral admixture since the ﬁrst decade of the 20th century. Initially, blast furnace slag and ﬂy ash were used in different parts of the worl

The concretes were mixed in a 150 L vertical-axis planetary mixer under lab-conditions (60% relative humidity and 21 ± 2 /C176C). Cylindrical specimens with 100 mm diameter and 200 mm height were cast in two layers and compacted using a vibration table for mechanical and durability tests. Also, the specimens were left to cure for 24 h under damp cloth, and then demolded and cured in a moisture controlled room with 100% relative humidity and 21 ± 1 /C176C. The rheological experiments were carried out in the par- allel plate BTRHEOM rheometer [18]. It was assumed that the fresh concrete behaves as a Bingham ﬂuid, for which a linear relation- ship exists between shear stress and shear velocity gradient. For the compressive strength ( fc) tests, two diametrically opposed lin- ear variation displacement transducers measured the axial defor- mation over a gage length of 100 mm at mid-height of the specimen. The average value of these measurements was used to control a Shimadzu UH-F1000kNI servohydraulic machine with displacement rate of 7.5 lm/min. Young’s modulus ( Ec) was calcu- lated considering the linearity of the stress–strain curve for stres- ses in the range of 50 /C210/C06Ec to 0.4 fc. The mechanical results were validated by Analysis of Variance and Duncan’s Multiple Range Tests and the differences were considered signiﬁcant with the probability P 6 0.05. The rapid chloride-ion permeability test was performed accord- ing to ASTM C1202-97 [19]. In this case, the specimen was cut out from the mid-portion of the cylindrical specimen of 100 mm diam- eter and 200 mm height, after 28 days of curing. Duplicate speci- mens were tested. The pore size distribution of reference and ternary mixtures in both types of concrete was performed using a Micrometics Autopore II 9215 mercury intrusion porosimeter assuming 0.485 N/m and 130 /C176as the surface tension and the con- tact angle between mercury and pore wall, respectively. The sam- ples of about 1 cm 3 were cut out using a diamond cutter from the mid-portion of cylinders with 100 mm diameter and 200 mm height after 180 days of curing in the moisture controlled room and drying at 45 /C176C until constant mass. Two experiments were carried out on a fully adiabatic calorimeter (see details in [10])t o investigate the exothermic behavior of reference and ternary con- ventional concretes until 30 days of hydration. The calorimeter works with a 200 L specimen with its temperature monitored by a thermocouple placed in its center. The temperature of the water that surrounds the specimen is controlled in order to match the temperature of the specimen. As result, any heat generated by hydration cement reactions results in an increase of temperature in the concrete and a corresponding temperature rise in the water. The initial concrete temperature was maintained at 20 ± 1 /C176C. 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Properties at the fresh state The partial replacement of Portland cement by both ashes causes different effects regarding to the concrete workability, as presented in Table 4. It can be observed in Table 3 that the dosage of superplasticizer in binary mixtures containing ultraﬁne RHA in comparison to the reference concretes is increased to maintain the slump consistency in the speciﬁed range. An opposite effect was observed for the binary mixtures with the ultraﬁne SCBA for both types of concrete. In this case, there is reduced consumption of superplasticizer to slumps between 130 and 170 mm. For

strengths of HS-TER and HS-REF. This behavior can be attributed mainly to the high pozzolanic activity of the RHA and the pro- nounced physical effects provided by the ultraﬁne particles, as was observed by Cordeiro et al. [14]. The increase or the lack of change in compressive strength of the ternary concretes, when compared to the results obtained for the reference concretes, can also be observed for long times of curing, as depicted in Fig. 3. After the evolution of strength with time until 180 days, both RHA mix- tures (CC-RHA20% and HS-RHA20%) present the highest compres- sive strengths. The ternary concretes present intermediate values between RHA and SCBA binary mixtures, mainly observed in the conventional concretes. Regarding to the effects of partial replacement of cement by the ashes on chloride-ion penetrability, it can be observed that when the ultraﬁne SCBA is used in the conventional concrete the electri- cal charge is reduced, although the classiﬁcation based on ASTM C1202-05 [19] in comparison to the CC-REF does not change. How- ever, when the ultraﬁne RHA is used an expressive reduction (about 90%) in the electric charge is observed and the ASTM classi- ﬁcation is modiﬁed to ‘‘low’’ chloride-ion penetrability. The con- ventional ternary concrete posses ‘‘moderate’’ penetrability with an electrical charge of 2862 C passing. It is interesting to note that all high-strength concretes with ashes (binary and ternary mix- tures) formulated present ‘‘very low’’ penetrability, while the HS- REF is classiﬁed as having ‘‘low’’ penetrability according to the standard. As expected, the HS-RHA20% has the lowest electric charge (261 C) and the HS-TER presents an intermediate behavior. The cumulative pore volume versus the pore diameter curves of reference and ternary concretes are indicated in Fig. 4 . The ﬁgure shows that the cumulative intruded volume for 3 nm is about 0.06 and 0.03 mL/g for conventional and high-strength concretes, respectively. Although the total volumes are almost the same for the two mixtures in both classes of concrete, important differences are observed when pores with different ranges of sizes are com- pared. Thus, it is possible to observe higher penetrations of mer- cury in the pore diameter range of 2–20 nm for the reference concretes, indicating reﬁnement of the pore structures in the ter- nary mixtures. This behavior contributes to the signiﬁcant reduc- tions observed in the electric charge values ( Table 4 ) of the Table 3 Mixture proportions of concretes in kg/m 3. large-scale use of agro-industrial ashes in concrete, which is its burning under uncontrolled conditions. The ﬁrst phase of the study was carried out involving fresh and hardened properties of the high-performance concretes containing an ultraﬁne SCBA from a sugar-alcohol plant. The results showed that the application of the bagasse ash allowed the production of high-performance con- crete with the same mechanical response with up to 20% mass replacement as the concrete prepared solely using Portland ce- ment. Such addition of the SCBA also resulted in improvements in rheology of concrete in the fresh state [10,11]. In the second phase of the study the inﬂuence of a RHA with high carbon content was evaluated in concretes produced with 0%, 10%, 15%, and 20% by mass of the cement replaced by the ash. For all levels of cement replacement, especially for the 20% replacement level, concretes containing RHA achieved superior performance in the mechanical and durability tests compared to the reference mixture. The workability of the concrete, neverthe- less, was reduced with the increase of cement replacement by the ash [12]. This present work consists of formulating a concrete that cou- ples the advantageous contribution of SCBA in concrete rheology to the improvement in the mechanical and durability properties of RHA, when they are used as partial cement replacements. Con- ventional and high-performance concrete were produced with bin- ary cementitious mixtures cement–SCBA or RHA and ternary mixtures cement–SCBA–RHA. Experiments were performed at the fresh and hardened states to evaluated rheological, mechanical, durability and calorimetric properties of the different concretes. 2. Materials Both SCBA and RHA were collected during the boiler cleaning operation of su- gar-alcohol and rice plants, respectively. The bagasse was burned in boilers at tem- peratures varying from 700 to 900 /C176C in the alcohol factory, situated in the State of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. The rice husk was burned in boilers at temperatures between 600 and 850 /C176C in the rice plant in the State of Santa Catarina, Brazil. The chemical compositions of the SCBA and RHA, determined by the X-ray ﬂuorescence method, and loss on ignition [13] are shown in Table 1. It is important to note that the SiO 2 content (78.3%) covers amorphous and crystalline silica from SCBA and some sand contamination, as it can be observed by the predominant presence of the quartz and cristobalite phases in diffraction patterns of the SCBA ( Fig. 1 ). High values of silica content (82.6%) and loss on ignition (11.9%), associated with the presence of resid- ual carbon, can be observed in the RHA. The difference between the loss on ignition values of SCBA and RHA samples is related with the burning conditions in the dif- ferent boilers of the alcohol and rice plants. Quantitative X-ray diffraction analyses were performed to determine the amorphous content of these ashes using Bruker’s Topas v. 3 software [11,12], which is based on the Rietveld method. The results indi- cated in Table 1 show that the SCBA presented 24 ± 4% of amorphous SiO 2, while it was estimated that the RHA is composed of 34 ± 1% of a-cristobalite and 49 ± 2% of amorphous SiO 2. Since the as-received SCBA and RHA samples consist, to a great extent, of coarse particles (with dimensions higher 200 lm), they were submitted to dry open circuit grinding using a vibratory mill. Grinding was carried out with 16.5 L of grinding media,

concretes with ashes in comparison with the references. Moreover, the physical and pozzolanic effects of the ultraﬁne particles of the ashes are certainly responsible to change in pore size distribution. In the previous studies carried out by the same authors the inﬂu- ence of SCBA [10] and RHA [12] on the reﬁnement of pore size was reported. These ﬁndings were also observed in other investiga- tions with RHA [20,21]. Fig. 5 shows the adiabatic temperature rise curves of the CC-REF and CC-TER. The results clearly show that the temperature of con- crete decreases with the replacement of cement by both ashes. For the reference mixture, the temperature rise is about 14 /C176C per 100 kg/m3 of concrete. When 40% of SCBA and RHA are incorpo- rated in the concrete, the temperature rise is about 12 /C176C per 100 kg/m3 of concrete and the maximum temperature rise is de- creased by 7.6 /C176C in comparison to the corresponding value ob- tained for the CC-REF. The time to reach high temperatures decreases with the use of both ashes. For instance, the time that corresponded to reaching 80% of the maximum temperature is equal to 42 h and 102 h for the CC-REF and CC-TER, respectively. Chusilp et al. [7] reported that the maximum temperature rise of concrete containing 10–30% bagasse ash was lower than the con- trol concrete evaluated under semi-adiabatic conditions. The reduction of extent of the adiabatic temperature rise and the delay in the time to reach high temperatures represent additional advan- tages of the application of concrete with high-contents of residual ashes in mass concrete construction for example. 5. Conclusions From the results described in this work, the following conclu- sions can be draw: /C15The partial replacement of Portland cement by both SCBA and RHA caused slight increase in yield stress and small reduction in plastic viscosity in both conventional and high-strength con- cretes due to the higher content of ultraﬁne particles comparing to the references. /C15The joint inﬂuence of both ashes allowed reaching a high level of cement replacement without the need for addition of large amounts of superplasticizer due to the positive effect of SCBA particles in the rheology. /C15The compressive strength of ternary conventional and high- strength concretes containing both types of ashes was either kept constant or increased when compared to the reference mixtures. These results show that RHA is more effective than SCBA in causing increase of compressive strength owing to its higher pozzolanic activity. /C15The ternary concretes presented lower electric charges (chloride- ion penetration test) and an intermediate behavior between the RHA and reference concretes. This change was consequence of the pozzolanic activity of both ashes and the reﬁnement in the pore size distribution in relation to the reference concretes. /C15Finally, the cement replacement by 40% of RHA-SBCA decreased signiﬁcantly the maximum adiabatic temperature rise of con- ventional concrete.
